coredata.dc:title: Microplasma nanoengineering of emission-tuneable colloidal nitrogen-doped graphene quantum dots as smart environmental-responsive nanosensors and nanothermometers
coredata.dc:creator: Kurniawan, Darwin
Weng, Ren-Jie
Setiawan, Owen
Ostrikov, Kostya (Ken)
Chiang, Wei-Hung
coredata.dc:description: Nitrogen-doped graphene quantum dots (NGQDs) with controlled emission properties are useful materials for fundamental research and applications. However, the scalable and sustainable production of NGQDs with defined nanostructures from biomass precursors remains elusive. Here a bottom-up microplasma synthesis of emission-controlled colloidal NGQDs used as the multifunctional nanosensors for temperature and selective metal ion sensing is demonstrated. The emission-tuneable NGQDs are synthesized by engineering the N-doping configurations and surface functional groups on the NGQDs surfaces by carefully controlling the plasma conditions and electrolyte chemistry. In situ optical emission and UV–Vis absorbance spectroscopy measurement reveals that the plasma-induced hydroxyl radicals and solvated electrons define the N-dopant configuration and surface functionalization of NGQDs, leading to emission-tuneable NGQDs. The emission-tuneable NGQDs are applied as multifunctional nanosensors for selective Fe3+, Cu2+, and Hg2+ detection revealing broad linear detection range (0.5–300 μM) and low limit of detection (LOD) of 47.9 nM for Hg2+ and sensitive temperature detection from 10 to 80 °C. The developed PL sensing method shows high throughput of 5000 detections per hour. Our work demonstrates the possibility of the plasma-enabled nanoengineering of NGQDs with controlled optical properties and a step towards sustainable and scalable synthesis of graphene nanomaterials from renewable bioresources.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Schematic illustration of the microplasma synthesis of chitosan-derived NGQDs with tuneable-emission and well-controlled structures and functionalities which can be exploited for Fe3+, Cu2+, Hg2+, and temperature sensing. (A colour version of this figure can be viewed online.)

Absorbance and PL emission-tuneable properties of the synthesized NGQDs. (a) Absorbance spectra of NGQDs synthesized using microplasmas at 9.6 mA fixed discharge current with different reaction time for 4.44 pH of electrolyte. (b) Normalized PL spectra of NGQDs synthesized using microplasmas at 9.6 mA fixed discharge current with different pH values of electrolytes. The corresponding PL maps of NGQDs synthesized using microplasmas at 9.6 mA fixed discharge current for 1 h with (c) 1.86 pH, (d) 1.95 pH, (e) 2.09 pH, (f) 2.24 pH, (g) 2.40 pH, (h) 2.53 pH, (i) 2.57 pH, (j) 2.69 pH, (k) 2.95 pH (l) 3.07 pH, (m) 3.28 pH, and (n) 4.44 pH of electrolytes. The scale bar represents the normalized PL intensity. (A colour version of this figure can be viewed online.)

TEM characterization of the synthesized NGQDs. TEM images of the synthesized NGQDs using microplasmas at 9.6 mA fixed discharge current with (a) 2.57 pH, (b) 2.69 pH, and (f) 4.44 pH of electrolytes. Scale bars: 5 nm. Insets show FFT images (bottom corner) and HRTEM images (top corner). Scale bars: 5 nm. Histograms of the synthesized NGQDs using microplasmas at 9.6 mA fixed discharge current with (a) 2.57 pH, (b) 2.69 pH, and (f) 4.44 pH of electrolytes. Particle diameters are assessed from the corresponding low magnification TEM images. (A colour version of this figure can be viewed online.)

XPS characterizations of synthesized NGQDs. HRXPS spectra of (a) C1s, (b) N1s, and (c)O1s for synthesized NGQDs using microplasmas at 9.6 mA fixed discharge current for 1 h with different pH values of electrolytes. NGQD1, NGQD2, and NGQD3 are synthesized NGQDs using microplasmas with pH of 2.57, 2.69, and 4.44 in electrolytes, respectively. (A colour version of this figure can be viewed online.)

Metal ions selectivity test of the three synthesized NGQDs. PL spectra of (a) NGQD1, (b) NGQD2, and (c) NGQD3 with the presence of 100 μM of different metal ions. (d) PL intensity ratios of NGQDs with the presence (I) to the absence of metal ions (I0). (A colour version of this figure can be viewed online.)

Metal ions sensing performances of the three synthesized NGQDs using microplasmas. PL spectra of 100 μg/mL of (a) NGQD1, (b) NGQD2, and (c) NGQD3 with the presence of different concentration of Fe3+, Cu2+, and Hg2+, respectively. The sensing performances of (d) NGQD1 for Fe3+sensing, (e) NGQD2 for Cu2+ sensing, and (f) NGQD3 for Hg2+ sensing. (A colour version of this figure can be viewed online.)

Temperature sensing performances of the three synthesized NGQDs using microplasmas. PL spectra of 500 μg/mL of (a) NGQD1, (b) NGQD2, and (c) NGQD3 at various temperatures. The temperature sensing performances of (d) NGQD1, (e) NGQD2, and (f) NGQD3 specimens. (A colour version of this figure can be viewed online.)
Summary of NGQD synthesis conditions and elemental ratios estimated using XPS analysis.
Summary of the NGQD nanosensor performance of metal ion and temperature sensing.

head.ce:title: Microplasma nanoengineering of emission-tuneable colloidal nitrogen-doped graphene quantum dots as smart environmental-responsive nanosensors and nanothermometers

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Chemical Engineering, National Taiwan University of Science and Technology, Taipei, 10607, Taiwan

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: National Taiwan University of Science and Technology

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Department of Chemical Engineering, National Taiwan University of Science and Technology, Taipei, 10607, Taiwan

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: School of Chemistry and Physics and QUT Centre for Materials Science, Queensland University of Technology (QUT), Brisbane, QLD, 4000, Australia

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: School of Chemistry and Physics and QUT Centre for Materials Science

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: School of Chemistry and Physics and QUT Centre for Materials Science, Queensland University of Technology (QUT), Brisbane, QLD, 4000, Australia

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Nitrogen-doped graphene quantum dots (NGQDs) with controlled emission properties are useful materials for fundamental research and applications. However, the scalable and sustainable production of NGQDs with defined nanostructures from biomass precursors remains elusive. Here a bottom-up microplasma synthesis of emission-controlled colloidal NGQDs used as the multifunctional nanosensors for temperature and selective metal ion sensing is demonstrated. The emission-tuneable NGQDs are synthesized by engineering the N-doping configurations and surface functional groups on the NGQDs surfaces by carefully controlling the plasma conditions and electrolyte chemistry. In situ optical emission and UV–Vis absorbance spectroscopy measurement reveals that the plasma-induced hydroxyl radicals and solvated electrons define the N-dopant configuration and surface functionalization of NGQDs, leading to emission-tuneable NGQDs. The emission-tuneable NGQDs are applied as multifunctional nanosensors for selective Fe3+, Cu2+, and Hg2+ detection revealing broad linear detection range (0.5–300 μM) and low limit of detection (LOD) of 47.9 nM for Hg2+ and sensitive temperature detection from 10 to 80 °C. The developed PL sensing method shows high throughput of 5000 detections per hour. Our work demonstrates the possibility of the plasma-enabled nanoengineering of NGQDs with controlled optical properties and a step towards sustainable and scalable synthesis of graphene nanomaterials from renewable bioresources.

ce:section.ce:para: The development of selective and sensitive metal ion detection is important to maintain and improve the water environment and biological systems. For example, a small intake of Hg2+ can lead to brain, kidney, and nervous system disorders [1–3]. On the other hand, Cu2+ and Fe3+ play an important role in pathological, physiological, and biological processes of living organisms [4,5], yet excessive intake of Cu2+ can cause gastrointestinal disturbance, liver or kidney damage, Wilson's, Parkinson's, and Alzheimer diseases [6,7]. While lack and excessive of Fe3+intake may cause anaemia, renal failure, cancer, heart and Parkinson's diseases [8,9]. Compared with conventional methods including atomic absorption spectrometry (AAS), inductively-coupled plasma mass spectrometry (ICP-MS), colorimetric, and electrochemical, fluorescence-based sensing is a simpler, more selective, sensitive, time and cost efficient, and convenient approach [10–12]. On the other hand, temperature is an important physicochemical indicator in the biochemical and physiological processes of living organisms. Moreover, with the current COVID-19 pandemic, fast and precise human's body temperature detection becomes not only the simplest but also the most essential procedure to prevent virus spreading. The latest nanothermometer technology involving fluorescence-based technique is particularly interesting due to their fast response, accuracy, and safety of remote handling [13,14]. Overall, it is challenging to detect Hg2+, Cu2+ and Fe3+ selectively as well as the temperature with simultaneously high selectivity and sensitivity using a single nanomaterial.

Here we address this issue by developing a new detection process based on plasma-nanoengineered emission-tuneable nitrogen-doped graphene quantum dots (NGQDs) with the controlled N dopant configuration and surface functionalities and reveal the unique underlying mechanisms of these detections leading to the outstanding performance. The GQDs has been selected as the fluorescent nanoprobes due to their exceptional properties including tuneable bandgaps, stable photoluminescence (PL), high carrier mobility, surface functionalities, large surface area, biocompatibility, and low toxicity [15,16]. Moreover, GQDs showed useful features for optoelectronics, nanocomposites, electrocatalysis for energy storage and conversion, chemical sensing, drug delivery and biomedical applications [16,17]. GQD properties, especially the emission bands and selectivity, are strongly depended on their structures and surface states [18–20]. Recently, doping GQDs with heteroatoms such as nitrogen (N), boron (B), sulphur (S), phosphorus (P) [21] has been shown as an effective approach to tune the emissions as well as the structural and electronic properties of GQDs. Among the doped GQDs, N-doped GQDs (NGQDs) are of particular interest for fundamental research and applications because they show modulated emissions and exhibit improved properties for catalysis [22,23], chemical sensing [24], and energy conversion [25].

There are two major strategies to synthesize NGQDs, including direct synthesis from carbon and N-containing precursors and post-processing by doping nitrogen into the pre-synthesized GQDs [26]. The post-processing usually involves harsh conditions, including high temperature and pressure, use of strong acids or bases, and long reaction times, to treat the mixtures containing nitrogen precursors and pre-synthesized GQDs [27,28]. For example, the pre-synthesized GQDs prepared via pyrolysis of citric acid at 200 °C were further treated hydrothermally with hydrazine at 180 °C for 12 h to dope the N atoms into the graphene core [28].

On the other hand, the direct synthesis is more favourable and can be realized using top-down and bottom-up approaches. Direct synthesis by top-down approach usually requires chemical, electrochemical, and mechanical exfoliations of N-doped 2D or 1D graphitic materials including graphene nanosheets and carbon nanotubes [29–31], while bottom-up approach involves carbonization, pyrolysis, or step-wise chemical reaction pathways of carbon and nitrogen-containing chemicals to produce NGQDs [16,17,26]. Yeh et al. synthesized nitrogen-doped graphene oxide quantum dots (NGOQDs) using a top-down approach, in which they first produced nitrogen-doped graphene in a flow of NH3 gas at 500 °C for 3 h, followed by oxidation via the modified Hummer's method to synthesize NGOQDs [23]. Meanwhile, Zhang et al. produced nitrogen-doped carbon quantum dots from the hydrothermal treatment of folic acid and ethylene glycol at 180 °C for 12 h [24]. Further emission tailoring of NGQDs can be achieved by size modification [32] and chemical functionalization [33]. Nevertheless, the aforementioned conventional processes usually require long reaction time, elevated temperatures, complicated precursors, and tedious separations [19,34,35].

Overall, a simple, scalable, sustainable and environmentally-friendly method to synthesize emission-controlled NGQDs from a single precursor is still not available. Here we demonstrate the microplasma-based process that satisfies the above requirements. Previous studies reported that microplasma is an effective technique for nanomaterials syntheses and processing in gas or liquid phase [36–40]. The high electron energy allows non-thermal dissociation of molecular gases to produce high concentrations of reactive radical species including electron, hydroxyl radicals (OH˙), O˙, O2¯˙, and NO˙ [41–43], leading to the formation of colloidal nanomaterials such as metal nanoparticles [37] and GQDs [39] in solutions. In addition, microplasma synthesis is amenable for large-scaled production in a facile manner since they can be operated under atmospheric condition without any vacuum equipment [40].

Here we report a novel and effective synthesis of the emission-tuneable NGQDs by nanoengineering the N dopant configuration and surface functionalities of GQD surfaces under ambient conditions using microplasmas (Fig. 1 ). We have previously demonstrated that NGQDs can be synthesized from chitosan dissolved with acetic acid solution under microplasma treatment. In this work, we further explore the plasma nanoengineering of the N dopant configuration of NGQDs by precisely controlling the chemistry conditions of the reactions during the synthesis (Fig. 1 and S1). Systematic in situ optical emission [Fig. S2(a)] and UV–Vis absorbance spectroscopy [Fig. S2(b)] measurement provides valuable chemical information during the plasma synthesis and reveal that the plasma-induced hydroxyl radicals and solvated electrons play a role for not only the NGQD synthesis but also the N dopant configuration and surface functionalization, leading to tuneable emission properties of NGQDs.

The utility of the synthesized emission-tuned NGQDs is demonstrated for two important applications. First, the NGQDs are used as multifunctional nanosensors for selective Fe3+, Cu2+, and Hg2+ detection with the broad linear detection range (0.5–300 μM) and the low limit of detection (LOD) of 47.9 nM for Hg2+ and sensitive temperature detection from 10 to 80 °C (Fig. 1). Compared with the conventional methods, the developed PL-based detection allows the rapid detection rate of 5000 runs per hour with the fixed emission wavelength measurement, showing the potential for a high-throughput sensing platform coupled with an automation system [44,45]. To the best of our knowledge, our work is the first report on the synthesis of emission-tuneable NGQDs with exceptional sensing properties for multifunctional and selective metal ion sensing, which is superior to the recently reported fluorescent carbon-based nanomaterials [3,5,9–12,46–54]. This work demonstrates the possibility of plasma nanoengineering of NGQDs with controlled optical properties and is a step towards sustainable and scalable synthesis of graphene nanomaterials from renewable bioresouces.

Chitosan (low molecular weight, averaged 120,000 Da), sodium hydroxide (NaOH, ≥98%) pellets were purchased from Sigma Aldrich Co., Ltd. Acetic acid glacial (CH3COOH, ≥99.7%) was purchased from Fischer Scientific. Hydrochloric acid (HCl, reagent grade, 37%) was purchased from Scharlau. Nitric acid (HNO3, analysis grade, 65%) was purchased from Pan Reac Appli Chem ITW Companies. Pt foil (99.95%, 20 mm
×
20 mm
×
0.1 mm) was purchased from Guv Team International Co., Ltd., Taiwan. Polytetrafluoroethylene (PTFE) membrane filter (M-PTFE047N022O, 47 mm diameter, 0.22 μm pore size) was obtained from Finetech Research and Innovation Co., Taiwan.

300 mg of chitosan was dissolved in 20 mL aqueous solution containing different acids with varying concentrations to adjust the electrolyte pH values (HCl: 100, 90, 80, and 70 mM; HNO3: 100, 90, 80, and 70 mM; AA: 1400, 1000, 600, and 100 mM) and further diluted two times by deionized (DI) water. The pH values of the final precursor solutions were recorded using a pH meter and the recorded pH values were used to represent the electrolytes pH. 10 mL of the obtained chitosan solution was then subjected to microplasma treatment [39] at 9.6 mA discharge current to synthesize NGQDs. The as-synthesized NGQDs solution was neutralized by adding 2 M NaOH solution. Then, acetone with the volume ratio of 1–1.5 of that NGQD solution was added to precipitate the unreacted chitosan. The suspension was subjected to centrifugation at 6000 rpm for 10 min to separate the unreacted chitosan precipitate and the mixture of NGQDs solution and acetone. The NGQDs solution was further filtered using vacuum filtration to make sure there was no chitosan leftover. The obtained solution was concentrated using rotary evaporator and followed by dialysis with DI water for 1 day to remove the salts produced during neutralization step. Then, the purified NGQDs solution was evaporated with rotary evaporator. Once all the solution had been evaporated, a small amount of ethanol was added to re-disperse the NGQDs stuck to the sample vial wall. Finally, the suspension was dried once again using rotary evaporator and fine NGQDs powder was obtained after drying in a vacuum chamber overnight. The solid precipitate was weighed and the yield could be estimated based on the formula below:

PL measurements of all liquid NGQDs dispersions were performed at room temperature using a commercial spectrometer (Horiba JobinYvon Nanolog-3 spectrofluorometer) equipped with an InGaAs NIR detector and a 20 nm bandpass for both emission and excitation. The PL spectra obtained were scaled according to the measured excitation power. The absorbance spectra of all materials were recorded using a JASCO V676 absorbance spectrophotometer with a matched pair of 1 cm path length quartz cuvettes. The corresponding solvents were used as baseline reference for every spectral measurement. MicroRaman measurement was conducted at room temperature with a JASCO 5100 spectrometer (laser excitation wavelength λ = 532 nm). The purified NGQDs and chitosan samples were drop-casted on Si wafers and dried on a hotplate at 35
°C
for 1 day before the measurements. The laser power was adjusted and maintained at 0.1 mW to avoid sample damages by the laser. Silicon wafer was used to calibrate a silicon wafer with a Raman shifts of 520 cm−1 peak was used to calibrated the Raman spectrometer before the measurement. XPS measurements were performed using an ESCALAB Xi+ (Thermo Fisher Scientific, United Kingdom) with monochromatic Al Kα X-ray radiation as the source gun type, a pass energy of 150.0 eV, and a beam size of 650 μm. TEM measurements were performed using a field emission gun TEM (FEI Tecnai™ G2 F-20 S-TWIN and JEOL JEM-2100F) with an accelerating voltage of 200 kV. TEM samples were prepared from solution dry-casting of the NGQDs colloidal solution onto formvar/carbon-coated copper grids (300 mesh, EM Resolutions Ltd.). MS measurements were performed using an electrospray ionization (ESI) MS (Orbitrap QE Plus Mass Spectrometer MS000100). Formic acid was used as the eluent for each MS measurements.

ce:section.ce:section-title: In situ OES and absorbance spectroscopy study
ce:section.ce:para: In situ OES measurements were conducted over the course of the reaction using a commercial spectrometer (Ocean Optics HR4000 spectrometer) with 100 ms integration time and 100 accumulations. The obtained spectra were integrated to represent the discharge information during the whole reaction. In order to see the features clearly, the integrated OES spectra were separated into three different sections including low, middle, and high wavelength regions. The low, middle, and high wavelength regions were normalized to N2 (336.54 nm), Hα (655.52 nm) and Ar peak (762.83 nm), respectively [refer to Fig. S7] [39]. In situ absorbance measurements were performed using a commercial spectrometer (Ocean Optics HR4000 spectrometer) coupled with a deuterium tungsten halogen light source (Ocean Optics DT-MINI-2-GS Deuterium Tungsten Halogen light source) and the measurement condition is 20 ms integration time and 50 accumulations.

All metal ions solutions were prepared by dissolving certain amounts of metal ion salts into a fixed volume of DI water to obtain 300 μM of different metal ions mother solutions. 300 μg/mL of different NGQDs solutions were prepared by dispersing the NGQD1, NGQD2, and NGQD3 into three separate sample vials filled with DI water. The NGQDs solutions were ultrasonicated for 10 min to obtain better NGQDs dispersion. For the metal ions selectivity, different metal ions solutions were mixed with different NGQDs solutions in separate sample vials to yield a final concentration of 100 μM of metal ions and 100 μg/mL of NGQDs. The mixtures were then incubated for 2 min prior to PL measurement at 330 nm excitation wavelength. For Fe3+, Cu2+, and Hg2+ sensing, similar procedures were conducted except that NGQD1 was used for Fe3+ sensing, NGQD2 for Cu2+, and NGQD3 for Hg2+ detections. The volume of each metal ion added to each NGQDs solution was adjusted to meet the desired final metal ion concentration for plotting.

Three different NGQDs solutions were prepared by dispersing the three NGQDs powder into three separate sample vials filled with fixed volume of DI water to yield 500 μg/mL of NGQDs solutions. Then, the NGQDs solutions were immersed in a water bath and the temperature of the water bath was adjusted to the desired value. The NGQDs solutions were incubated for 5 min prior to PLE mapping measurement. To find the optimum excitation wavelength, the PL intensity changes as a function of temperature were investigated based on different excitation wavelengths. The results were then plotted and fitted with the linear equation. The optimum excitation wavelength was determined based on the consideration of the obtained slope and R2 value.

Fig. 2 (a) shows the absorbance spectra of the synthesized NGQDs by treating chitosan electrolyte in 50 mM acetic acid (AA) aqueous solution with microplasmas. The plasma conditions were set at 9.6 mA fixed discharge current with varying reaction time from 10 to 80 min. The broad absorption bands exhibited in the spectra suggest the presence of various electronic states in the microplasma-synthesized NGQDs. The characteristic absorption peak at 281 nm for each spectrum is ascribed to π→π∗ transitions of sp2 carbon bonds and/or n→π∗ transitions of C–N bonds [39]. An absorption shoulder from 300 to 400 nm is also observed, which is attributed to n→π∗ transitions of aromatic CC bonds and nonbonding electrons of oxygen atoms in CO or C–O bonds [55]. The production yield of NGQDs is estimated using the 281 nm absorbance, which increases linearly with the plasma treatment time. The production rate of NGQDs and conversion are estimated to be 40 mg/h and 50%, respectively with a single microplasma treatment of a 10 mL chitosan precursor solution (7.5 mg/mL concentration), and can be further increased by optimizing the synthesis conditions (e.g. plasma current, voltage, power and treatment time) or using plasma arrays combined with continuous-flow microfluidic devices [56–60].

To study the pH effect on NGQDs synthesis, we first synthesized NGQDs using microplasmas from chitosan electrolytes prepared by varying the AA concentrations (50, 300, 500, and 700 mM), which subsequently making the solution pH became lower with increasing AA concentration. The plasma conditions were kept the same during the synthesis. The absorbance results indicate that less NGQDs are produced from electrolytes with lower pH [Fig. S3(a) and (b)]. It is suspected that high proton concentration may affect the plasma-liquid chemistry by neutralizing the eaq¯ species during the synthesis, which agrees with the previous study [61] and further implies that eaq¯ could be involved in the NGQD synthesis from chitosan (see in situ study discussion).

To support our hypothesis, we further utilized HCl and HNO3 with different concentrations for preparing the chitosan electrolytes with different pH values. Fig. 2(b) shows the normalized PL spectra of the synthesized NGQDs from electrolytes with different precursor pH. The data shows a 442 nm PL peak for NGQDs synthesized from electrolyte with 1.86 pH and is gradually red-shifted with increasing pH values of electrolytes, suggesting that the optical properties, especially the bandgaps, of the synthesized NGQDs were changed by altering the electrolyte pH during the plasma synthesis. To systematically study the effect of pH electrolyte on the emissions of the synthesized NGQDs, we further synthesized NGQDs from chitosan electrolytes with 12 different pH ranging from 1.86 to 4.44 using microplasmas and performed rigorous PL measurements. The detailed PL mappings for the 12 synthesized samples are shown in Fig. 2(c)–(n) as a function of excitation and emission wavelengths. The PL map in Fig. 2(c) indicates that 1.86 pH electrolyte produces NGQDs with one maximum PL intensity (shown in red) with 360 and 442 nm (2.81 eV) of excitation and emission, respectively. The broad range of the PL data can be attributed to various energy levels induced by surface functional groups, defects and edges states [62]. Moreover, the emission wavelength of NGQDs was found to be increasing with increasing the excitation wavelength, indicating a unique excitation-dependent emission property, which is similar to previous reports and is useful for several applications [63,64].

It is further noticed that the maximum PL intensity is red-shifted with increasing the electrolyte pH. For example, the electrolyte with pH 2.53 produces NGQDs with slightly red-shifted maximum PL intensity with 380 and 459 nm (2.70 eV) of the excitation and emission, respectively [Fig. 2(h)]. In comparison, NGQDs synthesized from the electrolytes with pH 2.95 and pH 4.44 show dramatic changes to the optical properties. Sample synthesized from the electrolyte with pH 2.95 is composed of NGQDs with the maximum PL intensity at 420 and 495 nm (2.51 eV) of the excitation and emission signals, respectively [Fig. 2(k)]. A more red-shifted maximum PL intensity is produced by the sample synthesized from the electrolyte with pH 4.44, showing the maximum PL intensity at 460 and 532 nm (2.33 eV) of the excitation and emission, respectively [Fig. 2(n)]. These results suggest that it is possible to not only control the emissions of NGQDs using microplasma synthesis by adjusting the proton concentrations and fragmentation degree (i.e., pH & pKa values), but also the chemical compositions in the chitosan electrolytes.

ce:section.ce:section-title: TEM, XPS, and Raman characterizations of the synthesized NGQDs
ce:section.ce:para: To reveal the possible factors to control the emissions of the as-synthesized NGQDs, detailed ex situ characterization including transmission electron microscopy (TEM), X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) and microRaman were performed. First, TEM was used to study the morphologies of the synthesized NGQDs. Three samples synthesized from three different electrolytes with the lowest acid concentrations were characterized. Table 1 summarizes their synthesis conditions. It is noted that the precursor electrolyte viscosity is strongly correlated with the chitosan molecular weight. The higher molecular weight makes the solution more viscous and vice versa. Besides dissolving the chitosan raw material, the acidic solution can also hydrolyse the long chain chitosan molecule. For complete chitosan dissolution and maintaining not too high viscosity of electrolyte solution, the least acid concentrations used were 35 mM for HCl and HNO3 and 50 mM for AA.

Representative TEM images [Fig. 3 (a)-(c)] show nanostructured particles with different sizes and no observable amorphous coatings. The Fast Fourier Transform (FFT) inset images reveal the crystalline structures of NGQDs synthesized by the microplasmas with different pH. The lattice fringes of the three different samples were confirmed to be 0.24 nm using high-resolution TEM (HRTEM), referring to the (1120) plane of graphene layer [65], as shown in the inset of Fig. 3(a)–(c). Beside showing the (1120) plane, it can also be observed that both NGQD1 and NGQD2 show other FFT patterns, indicating that the samples are polycrystalline in nature, similar with the work reported by Kumar et al. [66].

Different average particles sizes can be clarified based on the corresponding size distribution histograms depicted in Fig. 3(d)–(f). By normal distribution fitting of several different particles, the average sizes were found to be 3.79 ± 0.73 nm from 60 different particles [Fig. 3(d)], 4.36 ± 0.92 nm from 30 different particles [Fig. 3(e)], and 6.39 ± 1.01 nm from 50 different particles [Fig. 3(f)] for NGQD1, NGQD2, and NGQD3, respectively. The TEM results indicate that NGQDs with larger diameters are produced from higher electrolytes pH, supporting the PL data that NGQDs with larger diameters normally exhibit longer emission wavelengths which are proportional to the smaller bandgaps on the basis of the quantum confinement theory [18,20].

XPS was additionally used to probe the presence of nitrogen doping and functional groups on the synthesized NGQDs surfaces. Fig. S4 shows the scanned XPS spectra showing C1s, N1s, and O1s at 284.4, 400.4, and 532.4 eV, respectively for NGQD1, while 284.4, 399.4, and 531.4 eV, respectively for both NGQD2 and NGQD3. Table 1 summarizes the N/C and O/C ratios of the three representative samples. Both N/C and O/C ratios of the synthesized NGQDs are increasing with decreasing electrolyte pH (Table 1).

The bond configurations of the synthesized NGQDs were further studied using high-resolution XPS (HRXPS). The high-resolution C1s peaks of the three samples were deconvoluted into six different peaks, consisting of CC (284.4 eV) [67], C–C (284.8 eV) [68], C–N (285.6 eV) [23], C–O (286.2 eV) [69], CN (287.4 eV) [64], and COOH (288.6 eV) [70], as depicted in Fig. 4 (a). The existence of nitrogen doping in various configurations combined with other nitrogen containing functional groups in the resulted GQDs were confirmed from the deconvolution of high resolution N1s peak, revealing pyridinic N at 398.5 eV [66,71], amino N at 399.5 eV [68], pyrrolic N at 399.9 eV [72], graphitic N at 401.0 eV [66], and N–O bond at 402.8 eV [68] for the three NGQDs (Fig. 4).

The different nitrogen doping configurations are schematically shown in Fig. 1. The orange line with peak around 401.5 eV, ascribed to the nitrogenated adsorbents on the GQDs [73], which might originate from the air since the reaction was carried out in ambient conditions, as shown in the bottom and middle of Fig. 4(b). On the other hand, the high resolution O1s peak can be deconvoluted into several peaks at 530.9, 532.1, and 533.2 eV [64,70], corresponding to the C–OH, COOH, and C–O–C bonding configurations, respectively, for the three NGQDs samples. The additional O–N bond at 533.8 eV [68] was detected for both NGQD1 and NGQD2 samples, as shown in Fig. 4(c). The sodium (Na) peak observed around 536 eV can be attributed to the NaOH added into the system during the purification process.

The bonding percentage obtained from the C1s, N1s, and O1s peaks fitting are summarized in Table S1 while Fig. S5 presents the real bonding percentage per one NGQD particle (details can be seen in supporting information section S4). It is noticed that the pyrrolic N bond concentration increases with increasing electrolyte pH and only in the case of pH 4.44 that pyridinic N configuration is observed. Previous works demonstrated that pyridinic N and pyrrolic N generally can act as good electron donors while graphitic N as good electron acceptors owing to the high electronegativity of N atom compared with C atom [74,75]. Therefore, higher percentage of pyridinic N and pyrrolic N in NGQD3 increases the electron density of the delocalized system band leading to the bandgap narrowing [74,76]. As a result, there is a significant red-shift in NGQD3 (pH 4.44) compared with NGQD1 (pH 2.57) and NGQD2 (pH 2.69) samples [Fig. 2(b)]. The underlying tuneable PL mechanism could be attributed to the synergistic effect between the π→π∗ transition of sp2 carbon (size tuning) and n→π∗ transition of C–N (doping type tuning) and C–O bonds (functional groups tuning). Overall, the XPS analysis is consistent with both the PL and TEM results. The functional groups and nitrogen dopant distributions of the synthesized NGQDs were affected by the electrolyte pH, implying an intimate relationship between the NGQDs synthesis and the plasma-induced chemistry within the electrolytes.

MicroRaman analysis was conducted to further study the vibrational structures of the three NGQD samples together with their corresponding chitosan precursor solutions (Fig. S6). The chitosan solutions show broad D- and G-bands around 1352-1369 cm−1 and 1570-1591 cm−1, respectively [Fig. S6(a)]. In contrast, NGQDs samples not only exhibit sharp G-bands around 1581-1598 cm−1 that corresponds to graphene structure with high crystallinity [33], but also D-bands around 1344-1351 cm−1 that are attributed to the structural disorder in the graphene lattice caused by N-dopant, non-graphitic doping (i.e. pyridinic N and pyrrolic N), edges, and surface functional groups [Fig. S6(b)] [73]. Consequently, the averaged D- and G-band intensities ratio (ID/IG) for that NGQD3 is 1.02 ± 0.06 while NGQD1 and NGQD2 exhibit lower ratios of 0.59 ± 0.09 and 0.67 ± 0.08, respectively. In other words, as the chitosan electrolyte pH value decreases, the ID/IG of the synthesized NGQDs decreases as well, which can be attributed to higher graphitic N doping configurations and fewer functional groups (see XPS discussion).

Moreover, the TEM FFT analyses and HRTEM images also reveal the higher crystallinity in the NGQD1 sample compared with NGQD2 and NGQD3, which are in accordance to the microRaman results. On the basis of the TEM, XPS, and the Raman results, we hypothesize that the chitosan molecules were cleaved into smaller fragments at the lower electrolyte pH and then followed with reconstruction to form smaller NGQDs with higher N and O contents during the microplasma synthesis.

ce:section.ce:section-title: In situ OES and absorbance spectroscopy measurement of the plasma synthesis
ce:section.ce:para: To gain an insight into the mechanism of NGQDs synthesis, we conducted in situ OES and absorbance spectroscopy study. The effect of the plasma polarity on the NGQD synthesis from chitosan is first studied. Fig. S10 shows the plasma polarity experimental setup. The negative discharge (ND) shows the higher absorbance and more red-shifted maximum PL spectra after 30 min of the plasma reaction at 9.6 mA discharge current, compared with the positive discharge (PD), shown in Fig. S11.

This result is attributed to the fact that chitosan is positively charged in acidic conditions due to the protonation of the amine group and is attracted to the cathode side where the electrons are coming from. Since the microplasma is generated at the cathode side in the ND case, chitosan is attracted to the plasma-liquid interface where the generation of reactive species with high density can be expected. Hence, the higher production and faster growth rates of NGQDs can be expected in the ND case. Our result is consistent with the previous work which found the plasma polarity to be a critical factor in the GQDs synthesis from organosulfate precursors [77].

The reactive species generated by the microplasmas in the ND case were further monitored using in situ OES. Details about the peak assignments for each species can be seen in Supporting S6 section. Importantly, OH (A2Σ+→X2Π) emission lines are observed at 306–309 nm, as shown in Fig. S7(a). This result indicates that the radicals and electrons generated by the plasma can dissociate water upon impact, producing a high density of OH radicals, which is in agreement with the previous work [43]. Indeed, OH radicals are highly reactive for cleaving the β-1-4-glycosidic bonds within chitosan [78], leading to the generation of small fragments or moieties. The fragmentation of chitosan was evidenced by mass spectroscopy (MS) analyses [Fig. S8]. More chitosan decomposition is observed with the longer plasma reaction time and lower electrolyte pH (complete discussion can be seen in Supporting S7 section). This result infers that it is plausible to control the fragmentation degree of chitosan by controlling the electrolyte pH to yield emission-tuned NGQDs.

Another factor in the precursor conversion process is H2O2, a long-lived oxygen reactive specie, which can be generated from the recombination of OH radicals during the plasma synthesis [43,79,80]. Moreover, H2O2 can easily react with organic compounds. It is likely that the increasing concentration of H2O2 produced at the latter stage of the plasma reaction may affect the formation of NGQDs.

To further probe this hypothesis, experiments with external addition of H2O2 with different concentrations were additionally performed complemented with both in situ absorbance as well as ex situ absorbance and PL spectroscopies measurements (see Supporting S8 for details). The integrated absorbance intensity is clearly observed with 1 min plasma synthesis and drastically increases after 2 min reaction for the chitosan electrolyte with 50 mM and without adding H2O2, yet the production rate become slower after 5 min reaction for the former case. Moreover, the integrated absorbance intensity increases slower with the presence of 100 mM H2O2 in the electrolyte.

On the basis of the above results, it can be inferred that H2O2 could affect the yield and size of NGQDs by inhibiting the growth process. Previous works show that plasma-generated electrons or solvated electrons (eaq ̶) might interact with the sample solution, initiating, and accelerating the nucleation and formation of GQDs, carbon dots (CDs), or N-doped CDs [39,80–84]. Since H2O2 is also a well-known eaq ̶ scavenger, the addition of H2O2 into the precursor electrolyte further supports the crucial role of eaq ̶ in the synthesis. Nonetheless, further study still needs to be conducted to understand how H2O2 as well as eaq ̶ impact the NGQD formation.

Based on the results, the plausible mechanism of microplasma synthesis of NGQDs from chitosan is proposed, as shown in Fig. S12. First, the chitosan was dissociated into small fragments by OH radicals produced by the plasma interacting with the electrolyte. The resultant fragments interacted with electrons and reactive species from the plasma to generate radical species. It is known that the radical species can undertake radical-radical recombination, leading to the establishment of C–C bond or disproportionation, leading to the CC bond formation [39,85–88]. Therefore, the formation of NGQDs might proceed through the sequence of bond scission and reconstruction reactions. It is suggested that smaller fragments were generated from chitosan in lower electrolytes pH, leading to the synthesis of NGQDs with the smaller diameters or shorter PL emission wavelengths. By balancing the concentrations of hydroxyl radicals at the plasma-liquid interfaces via adjusting the pH of chitosan electrolyte, it is possible to control the emission of NGQDs produced from chitosan using microplasmas. It is also possible to yield NGQDs with different functionalities by modifying the acid type used in the electrolyte.

To reveal the effect of microplasma-controlled functionalization on NGQDs synthesis, metal ions selectivity test was conducted using three different NGQDs (NGQD1, NGQD2, NGQD3). Fig. 5 shows the effect of 100 μM metal ions on the PL properties of the three NGQDs. The metal ions selectivity was investigated by taking PL intensities ratio of NGQDs with the absence of metal ions (I
0 ) and the presence of metal ions (I), as presented in Fig. 5(d). One can see that the NGQD3 sample is the most selective toward Hg2+ and therefore can be exploited for Hg2+ sensing application. The interference of Cu2+ can be solved by adding a masking or chelating agent which can only interact with Cu2+ and not with Hg2+ [11], thus enabling the selective and sensitive detection of Hg2+. Meanwhile, NGQD2 shows a good selectivity toward Cu2+ compared with other metal ions, hence is applicable as a Cu2+ sensor. NGQD1, on the other hand, shows good selectivity toward Fe3+ and Cu2+. Similar to NGQD3 case, the interference of Cu2+ in the NGQD1 case can be addressed by the addition of either masking or chelating agent to allow selective detection toward Fe3+only. Noted that the difference in the PL emission presented in Figs. 2(b) and Figure 6 (a)-(c) can be attributed to the effect of concentration-dependent emission of NGQDs [89–91].

The selectivity underpinned by the different PL quenching degree upon the addition of different metal ions can be explained by two fundamental processes, mainly the adsorption of metal ions on the NGQD surface, followed by the electron transfer from the NGQDs to the metal ions leading to PL quenching. The adsorption of metal ions on the NGQDs surface is determined by the functional groups on the NGQD surface. Importantly, the affinity of each metal ion to certain functional groups might be different. Indeed, Hg2+ have strong affinity towards –OH group, while Cu2+ tend to attach to –COOH groups and are also able to interact with electron-donating N-dopants on GQD surface (pyridinic N and pyrrolic N sites) [92–94]. The results in Table S1 reveals that ∼50% of the oxygen functionalization is due to the –OH groups, while the data presented in Fig. S5 further confirm that the higher presence of –OH groups compared with NGQD1 and NGQD2 samples, may be the key reason for the high selectivity of NGQD3 towards the Hg2+ detection.

Meanwhile, although Cu2+ have strong affinity towards –COOH, pyridinic N, and pyrrolic N sites, it does not exclude the possibility that Cu2+ can also interact with –OH, C–O–C, and amino groups; however, the adsorption rates in these cases are expected to be lower [94]. Based on the bonding percentage shown in Fig. S5, we further determine the percentage of pyridinic N, amino N, pyrrolic N, –OH, –COOH, and C–O–C sites for all three NGQD types. Collectively, we have found that 29.60, 31.25, 33.19% of all possible binding sites in NGQD1, NGQD2, and NGQD3, respectively, can be used for the interaction with Cu2+. This result is in an excellent agreement with the order of selectivity towards Cu2+, where NGQD3 is the most sensitive, followed by NGQD2 and then NGQD1 particles. Since NGQD3 is also sensitive toward Hg2+, NGQD2 is better suited as the Cu2+ probe.

Once the metal ions have interacted and bonded with the surface functionalities of NGQDs, the PL quenching mechanism takes place. It has been reported that during the light absorption, the electron will be excited from the highest occupied molecular orbital (HOMO) to the lowest unoccupied molecular orbital (LUMO). However, upon the bonding of a metal ion onto the surface of GQDs, an electron from the HOMO of GQDs is transferred to the metal ion during the absorption process. Consequently, the electron-hole recombination is less likely to happen, inducing fluorescence quenching [95]. However, the in the case of Fe3+ detection, inner filter effect (IFE) phenomenon might hinder the above process and possibly be the dominant PL quenching mechanism, as shown in Fig. S13. The PL excitation (PLE) spectra of the three NGQDs significantly overlap with the absorption spectrum of Fe3+. When the PLE and the absorption spectra of a probe and analyte overlap substantially, the IFE process becomes likely [96]. Based on this result, it is plausible that despite the possibility of Fe3+ to interact and combine with the functional groups of GQDs/NGQDs, there is a strong tendency that the IFE can interfere with or even impede the electron transfer process if the PLE spectrum of GQDs/NGQDs overlaps substantially with the absorption spectrum of the analyte. Therefore, the selectivity order of the three NGQDs toward Fe3+ is relatively similar, yet NGQD1 which is only reactive toward Cu2+ and Fe3+ can be used as a Fe3+ probe by masking the Cu2+ with some chelating agents [93], such as ethylenediaminetetraacetic acid (EDTA) during the detection process. However, the PL mechanism of GQDs is presently still unclear, thus more studies are needed to validate the mechanisms proposed in this work.

Fig. 6 (a)–(c) shows the PL spectra of 100 μg/mL NGQD1, NGQD2, and NGQD3 with the addition of different concentration of Hg2+, Cu2+, and Fe3+, respectively. The metal ion sensing performance was investigated by plotting PL intensity changes (I
0
/I) as a function of metal ion concentration [Fig. 6(d)–(f)]. The PL intensity changes of NGQD1 exhibit a linear relationship with the concentration of Fe3+ from 1 to 300 μM with a high R2 value of 0.9983 [Fig. 6(d)]. In Cu2+ and Hg2+ sensing cases, two linear lines were required to fit the PL intensity changes of NGQD1 and NGQD2 as a function of Cu2+ and Hg2+, respectively. For NGQD2 as Cu2+ sensor, the first linear line starts from 0.5 to 10 μM and followed with 10–100 μM for the second linear line [Fig. 6(e)]; while for NGQD3 as Hg2+ sensor, the first linear line starts from 0.5 to 60 μM and followed with 60–100 μM for the second linear line [Fig. 6(f)].

The two linear ranges observed in NGQD2 for Cu2+ [Fig. 6(e)] and NGQD3 for Hg2+ sensing [Fig. 6(f)] might be due to different interactions between the NGQDs and the metal ions at lower and higher concentrations of metal ions. Recent study [11] suggested that Cu2+ and Hg2+ can interact with the functional groups from multiple carbon dots (CDs) to form molecular complexes of CDs-metal structure, resulting in PL quenching. However, the two linear lines corresponding to the Cu2+ and Hg2+ detection behave quite differently. In the case of NGQD2 for Cu2+ detection, the first linear line is steeper than the second linear line, while this trend is the opposite for the case when NGQD3 is used for Hg2+ detection. The steeper line indicates the higher sensitivity towards the relevant ion. Hence, it is possible that the NGQD-metal complex will be formed directly after the addition of Cu2+ to the colloidal NGQD2 even at low concentration of Cu2+. On the contrary, the addition of a low concentration of Hg2+ to the colloidal solution of NGQD3 results in the interaction between Hg2+ and functional groups of NGQDs, followed with NGQD-metal complex formation only when the concentration of Hg2+ is sufficiently high. The different interaction behavior between the two metal ions with the corresponding NGQDs can be attributed to the different affinity of metal ions toward oxygen containing functional groups. In this case, Cu2+ have the stronger affinity to the functional groups of NGQD compared with Hg2+.

The limit of detections (LODs) for the three NGQDs were estimated to be 66.7, 146.5, and 47.9 nM for NGQD1 as Fe3+ sensor, NGQD2 as Cu2+ sensor, and NGQD3 as Hg2+ sensor, respectively (S/N = 3). The feasibility of our microplasmas technique for the synthesis of emission-controlled NGQDs provides an excellent synthesis method, allowing selective detections for three different metal ions. To the best of our knowledge, our work is the first report to synthesize emission-tuneable NGQDs with exceptional sensing properties for multifunctional and selective metal ion sensing, which is superior to the recently reported fluorescent carbon-based nanomaterials [3,5,9–12,46–54]. Subsequently, Fe3+, Cu2+, and Hg2+ were added to tap water to test the applicability of NGQD1, NGQD2, and NGQD3 for ion detection in real water samples, respectively [54]. Table S3 shows that the concentration of metal ions estimated from the calibration line in [Fig. 6(d)–(f)] are very close to the concentration of metal ions introduced into the tap water. The low relative standard deviation (RSD) values (<4%) prove that the synthesized NGQDs are promising for selective metal ion sensing in real water samples.

We further investigated the possibility of the synthesized NGQDs for temperature sensing. By exploiting the excitation-dependent emission properties of the synthesized NGQDs, the temperature sensing performance can be rigorously analysed based on different excitation wavelengths to find the optimum excitation wavelength. The optimum wavelengths were found to be 370 nm for NGQD1 and 340 nm for both NGQD2 and NGQD3 after considering the slope value and R2 value which indicates the sensitivity and linearity, respectively (see Fig. S14). Fig. 7 (a)-(c) shows the PL spectra of NGQD1, NGQD2, and NGQD3 at different temperature excited under the optimum excitation wavelength for each case. The PL intensity at room temperature (25 °C) was taken as a basis and labelled with I
0 , while the PL intensity at different temperature was labelled with I. The PL intensity changes of the three NGQDs exhibit a linear relationship with temperature variations from 10 to 80 °C [Fig. 7(d)–(f)]. The sensing results are summarized in Table 2 .

From the above result, it can be observed that NGQD2 is the best sample for temperature sensing application, inferred by the highest slope and R2 value. It has been reported that the diminishing of PL intensity at higher temperature is attributed to the occurrence of non-radiative decay processes [90]. The emissive traps in the NGQDs due to C–O–C and COOH groups become more activated at an elevated temperature, promoting more non-radiative channels and inducing PL quenching via non-radiative relaxation process [90,97]. This hypothesis is supported with the XPS bonding percentage data where NGQD2 has the highest percentage of C–O–C and COOH groups in total (see Table S1). In addition, the PL quantum yield (PLQY) of the three samples were estimated to be 4.68% and 1.74% for NGQD1 and NGQD2 using quinine sulfate as standard, and 30.10% for NGQD3 using rhodamine 6G as standard [98], as shown in Fig. S15. The low PLQY of NGQD2 is a consequence of the high surface emissive trap content which further supports the above hypothesis. The high PLQY of NGQD3 can be related to the high content of pyrrolic N sites which contribute to the suppression of the emission traps formed by the carboxylic and epoxy groups [99,100].

In summary, we have demonstrated a bottom-up, one-step synthesis of NGQDs with tuneable emission using atmospheric-pressure microplasmas. Ex situ materials characterization indicates that the NGQDs with tuned emissions can be synthesized through microplasma treatment by controlling the pH of the chitosan electrolytes. In situ spectroscopy study suggests that the hydroxyl radicals and solvated electrons eaq ¯ are important reactive species for the synthesis of NGQDs during the microplasma interaction with the electrolyte. Moreover, emissions of the synthesized NGQDs can be tuned by adjusting the chitosan electrolyte pH which may affect the concentration of OH radicals and eaq¯ in the solution. On the basis of the experimental result, the possible NGQD synthesis pathway is proposed. With the assistance of OH radicals and eaq¯, the long chain chitosan biopolymer is fragmented into shorter chains then followed by bond reconstruction to form NGQDs. It is possible to not only control the emissions of NGQDs from chitosan using microplasmas but also the functionalities by adjusting the electrolyte pH and composition to indirectly or directly influence the hydroxyl radicals and eaq¯ production at the plasma-liquid interface. Different functionalities of NGQDs can strongly affect the possible applications of the end-products and thus it is possible to engineer the NGQDs synthesis to suit the desired properties for specific applications with good performances. The three synthesized NGQD specimens are useable for selective metal ions detections, comprising of NGQD1 for Fe3+ detection from 1 to 300 μM with LOD of 66.7 nm, NGQD2 for Cu2+ detection from 0.5 to 100 μM with LOD of 146.5 nm, and NGQD3 for Hg2+ detection from 0.5 to 100 with LOD of 47.9 nm. In addition, among the three synthesized NGQDs, NGQD2 can be used as the most sensitive temperature sensor from 10 to 80 °C. Our work provides a fundamental understanding on the NGQD synthesis using microplasmas and valuable information of plasma electrochemistry for the controlled synthesis of colloidal nanomaterials.
Additional UV–Vis absorption, PL, XPS, Raman, mass, in situ OES and absorbance. This material is available free of charge via the Internet.
